perturbation.original_text: Confidential Information shall not include any of the foregoing items to the extent that (1) they are or have become publicly known and made generally available through no wrongful act of Receiving Party, (2) they were known to Receiving Party prior to disclosure by Disclosing Party, as evidenced by pre-existing written records promptly provided to Disclosing Party by Receiving Party, (3) they were disclosed to Receiving Party without an obligation of confidentiality by a third party having a lawful right to make such disclosure, or (4) they were developed by Receiving Party without use or aid of Disclosing Party's Confidential Information, and (ii) the results of the Research (including the contents of each Report and any Inventions) shall be deemed to be LBIO's Confidential Information, subject to MD Anderson's right to publish any Research data and information as set forth in and in accordance with Section 6.4, MD Anderson's right to use any Inventions (and any Work) as set forth in and in accordance with Section 7.2, and MD Anderson's right to use any Research data and information for internal research, academic, and non-commercial patient care purposes prior to publication or public disclosure and for any purpose thereafter.
perturbation.changed_text: Confidential Information shall not include any of the foregoing items to the extent that (1) they are or have become publicly known and made generally available through any act of Receiving Party, (2) they were known to Receiving Party prior to disclosure by Disclosing Party, without the need for written records, (3) they were disclosed to Receiving Party with an obligation of confidentiality by a third party, or (4) they were developed by Receiving Party with use or aid of Disclosing Party's Confidential Information, and (ii) the results of the Research (including the contents of each Report and any Inventions) shall be deemed to be public information, subject to LBIO's right to claim proprietary rights.
perturbation.explanation: The definition of 'Confidential Information' is altered to include information publicly known due to the Receiving Party's actions, removes the written record requirement for pre-existing knowledge, allows confidential third-party disclosures to nullify confidentiality, includes information developed WITH Disclosing Party's Confidential Information, and designates research results as public. These changes undermine standard confidentiality protections.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Theft of Trade Secrets; Texas Penal Code § 31.05
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text significantly weakens the definition of confidential information, potentially allowing for the misappropriation of trade secrets without legal recourse. By stating that information developed with the Disclosing Party's confidential information is not confidential, this directly contradicts the protection afforded to trade secrets under the Texas Penal Code, which criminalizes the unauthorized disclosure or use of trade secrets, regardless of whether the information was directly stolen or derived from stolen information. This creates an environment where trade secrets are not adequately protected.

The following section was amended by the 89th Legislature. Pending publication of the current statutes, see H.B. 127 , 89th Legislature, Regular Session, for amendments affecting the following section. Sec. 31.05. THEFT OF TRADE SECRETS. (a) For purposes of this section: (1) "Article" means any object, material, device, or substance or any copy thereof, including a writing, recording, drawing, sample, specimen, prototype, model, photograph, microorganism, blueprint, or map. (2) "Copy" means a facsimile, replica, photograph, or other reproduction of an article or a note, drawing, or sketch made of or from an article. (3) "Representing" means describing, depicting, containing, constituting, reflecting, or recording. (4) "Trade secret" means the whole or any part of any scientific or technical information, design, process, procedure, formula, or improvement that has value and that the owner has taken measures to prevent from becoming available to persons other than those selected by the owner to have access for limited purposes. (b) A person commits an offense if, without the owner's effective consent, he knowingly: (1) steals a trade secret; (2) makes a copy of an article representing a trade secret; or (3) communicates or transmits a trade secret. (c) An offense under this section is a felony of the third degree. Acts 1973, 63rd Leg., p. 883, ch. 399, Sec. 1, eff. Jan. 1, 1974. Amended by Acts 1993, 73rd Leg., ch. 900, Sec. 1.01, eff. Sept. 1, 1994. Sec. 31.06. PRESUMPTION FOR THEFT BY CHECK OR SIMILAR SIGHT ORDER. (a) If the actor obtained property or secured performance of service by issuing or passing a check or similar sight order for the payment of money, when the issuer did not have sufficient funds in or on deposit with the bank or other drawee for the payment in full of the check or order as well as all other checks or orders then outstanding, it is prima facie evidence of the issuer's intent to deprive the owner of property under Section 31.03 (Theft) including a drawee or third-party holder in due course who negotiated the check or order or to avoid payment for service under Section 31.04 (Theft of Service) (except in the case of a postdated check or order) if: (1)
perturbation.justification: The changed text significantly weakens confidentiality protections, potentially enabling misappropriation of trade secrets without legal recourse. The modification stating that information developed with the Disclosing Party's confidential information is *not* confidential, directly contradicts the protection afforded to trade secrets. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'trade secret' as information with value that the owner has taken measures to prevent from becoming available and states that a person commits an offense if, without the owner's effective consent, he knowingly steals a trade secret; makes a copy of an article representing a trade secret; or communicates or transmits a trade secret.

perturbation.original_text: MD Anderson represents, warrants, and, to the extent applicable, covenants, that: (a) MD Anderson and all of its Representatives maintain as current the applicable licenses and permits, including medical practitioner licenses as required by the applicable national, state, and/or local licensing body and that no license or permit has been revoked, limited, suspended, or otherwise modified.
perturbation.changed_text: MD Anderson represents, warrants, and, to the extent applicable, covenants, that: (a) MD Anderson and all of its Representatives maintain or will at some point obtain the applicable licenses and permits, including medical practitioner licenses as required by the applicable national, state, and/or local licensing body regardless of if any license or permit has been revoked, limited, suspended, or otherwise modified.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause warrants that all licenses are current and unblemished. The modified version suggests that the licenses might not be current and that revocations, limitations, or suspensions are irrelevant. This introduces uncertainty and potential non-compliance.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Texas Medical Board Rules, specifically Rule §164.053. Standards of Care
perturbation.law_explanation: This change would contradict the Texas Medical Board Rules. Specifically, Rule §164.053 mandates that physicians adhere to certain standards of care. Maintaining current, unblemished medical licenses is essential for demonstrating adherence to these standards. Allowing for revoked, limited, or suspended licenses directly contradicts the requirement for physicians to be qualified and authorized to practice medicine, potentially endangering patients and violating regulatory requirements.

perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a contradiction by stating that licenses might not be current and that revocations, limitations, or suspensions are irrelevant. This directly opposes the original clause's warranty that all licenses are current and unblemished. The Law Explanation states that this change contradicts Texas Medical Board Rules, specifically Rule §164.053, which mandates adherence to certain standards of care. Allowing for revoked, limited, or suspended licenses contradicts the requirement for physicians to be qualified and authorized to practice medicine. The provided scraped law snippet, while not explicitly detailing Rule §164.053, confirms the existence and relevance of the Texas Medical Board Rules and their enforcement. Therefore, the modified text creates a legal contradiction by potentially allowing non-compliance with required medical licensing standards.

perturbation.original_text: LBIO may assign or transfer this Agreement without the prior written consent of but with written notice to MD Anderson promptly following consummation of the relevant transaction. MD Anderson hereby acknowledges and agrees that the rights and obligations hereunder are of a personal nature and, therefore, neither this Agreement nor any right or obligation contained within shall be assignable, transferable or delegable in whole or in part by MD Anderson and MD Anderson shall not, without the prior written consent of LBIO, sub-contract or otherwise engage any consultant or other third party to perform any of MD Anderson's activities or obligations under this Agreement or any Study Order.
perturbation.changed_text: LBIO may assign or transfer this Agreement without any notice to MD Anderson. MD Anderson may also assign or transfer this Agreement without any notice to LBIO, and the rights and obligations hereunder are not of a personal nature, therefore, this Agreement nor any right or obligation contained within shall be assignable, transferable or delegable in whole or in part by MD Anderson with no approval needed, sub-contract or otherwise engage any consultant or other third party to perform any of MD Anderson's activities or obligations under this Agreement or any Study Order.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause restricts MD Anderson's ability to assign the agreement without LBIO's consent. The modified version eliminates the need for consent, thus diminishing LBIO's control over who MD Anderson works with and potentially impacting the quality and integrity of the research.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Requirements for Research Misconduct and Protection of Human Subjects, 45 CFR § 94
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text contradicts federal regulations concerning research misconduct and protection of human subjects (45 CFR § 94). These regulations require institutions to maintain control and oversight over research activities, especially when human subjects are involved. By allowing MD Anderson to freely assign the agreement and subcontract research activities without LBIO's consent, this significantly reduces the ability to ensure compliance with ethical and regulatory standards. The federal regulations require a certain degree of oversight that are being contradicted.

perturbation.justification: The original text restricts MD Anderson's ability to assign the agreement without LBIO's consent, while the modified version eliminates this need. The Law Explanation states that the modified text contradicts federal regulations (45 CFR § 94) concerning research misconduct and protection of human subjects by reducing the ability to ensure compliance with ethical and regulatory standards. Scraped Law Snippet 2 mentions the Office for Human Research Protections (OHRP) which publishes policy and regulatory guidance materials to assist the research community in conducting ethical research that is in compliance with the HHS regulations, indicating that there is an oversight component for research that this change diminishes.
